title: Frequency Counter — Gate-Time Pulse Counting Method
content: A frequency counter measures the number of input pulses (N) that occur during a precise gate time window (T_gate). Frequency f = N / T_gate. Resolution improves with longer gate times: at T_gate = 1 s, resolution = 1 Hz; at T_gate = 10 s, resolution = 0.1 Hz. Reciprocal counting (measure period for low frequencies) improves accuracy for f < 1 kHz.
extended_content: Hardware implementation: MCU hardware timer (e.g., Arduino Timer1 in counter mode) counts rising edges on an external pin while a software or Timer0 gate controls the count window. Gate time accuracy depends on MCU crystal accuracy (typically ±20–50 ppm for ceramic resonators, ±10 ppm for crystals). For precision > 1 ppm, use a GPS-disciplined oscillator or TCXO reference. Maximum countable frequency: limited by MCU GPIO response and timer prescaler — typically 5–10 MHz on an Arduino Uno, up to 80 MHz on ESP32 PCNT peripheral. Reciprocal method: measure the period T of each pulse using a high-resolution timer; f = 1/T. Better for low frequencies (< 1 kHz) where gate-time method has poor resolution. Applications: RPM measurement from encoder pulses, audio frequency tuner, crystal frequency testing, flow meter pulse counting.
units: f: Hz, N: integer, T_gate: s
source: Hewlett-Packard Application Note 200; Atmel AVR135: Frequency Counter using Input Capture Unit
